title: How We Made $148,467 From YouTube AGAIN

transcript.text: Just about 3 days ago, this channel from our portfolio crossed a million followers. We've run it for 6 months, got 1.4 billion views, and generated $130,000 in revenue with 30k of that coming in the last 28 days. And the main thing is we didn't get hit with inauthentic content. We didn't steal someone else's stuff. Instead, we built an original brand using what I call niche bending and unique team structure. And in this video, I'm going to show you exactly how the entire system works so you can replicate it. And I'm not going to guarantee you anything. Most people will watch it and do nothing, but if you are in 1% of action takers, you're going to get a lot of sauce from this video. And if you don't know who I am, my name is Tim. I've run Art of War from zero to over 1.6 million followers and $300,000 in revenue in about a year, and then started investing in other channels, scaling multiple of them to five figures months, and currently we run a portfolio that generates over 700k per year. Now, in this video, we are uncovering the secrets of one of them where we generated 1.4 billion views and made 130k in the last 6 months. We built it together with one of the Art of YouTube clients, and if you want to check his side of the story, he already posted a channel reveal and basically his strategy behind this channel. Someone who came up with the niche and obviously ran it as an operator. Seb posted it on his channel. I'll link this video down below if you want to check it out. Go do it because he's an absolute killer. Now, the five things that I'm going to walk you through in this video. Number one is the niche and obviously how Seb found it. Number two is the scripting formula that prints views for us. Number three, the first three videos and how we generated $12,000 from the first three videos. Then, how we went through the dip and why channel depth and what happened and how we got out of it and obviously the unfair advantage that each and every one of you can get after watching this video. Now, before we get into all of this, this is actually the second time I've pulled this off. Right here, you can see I already have one of the gold play buttons because Art of War was the first time we actually tested and tried this strategy. This is the channel where we generated 4 billion views and uh 300k in revenue in about a year, 1.6 million followers and this is where we dialed in the framework that we then used to build the channel together with Sap. Because Sap has a very interesting background and with this background it's actually very easy to crush it on YouTube when you do it right because Sap was burning out on commentary. He used to run multiple channels, they went up and down like commentary always does. He would steal someone else's content, re-edit it add some sound effects and some arrows and some fast-paced editing, but at the end of the day it would still be stealing and obviously he would get some strikes here and there. The niches were always saturated, there's always thousands of players trying to do the same stuff and there are no differentiators meaning you always share the pie with thousands of other players. And he wanted to launch and run a branded high-quality channel. The difference between those, if you're new, is when you create branded content, you A create it from scratch meaning every single piece of that is going to be original and then B you're building not only the channel but the brand around something. You're using your signature subtitles, sometimes you have a channel character, the one that you're making popular. All of that combined gives you more opportunities with brand deals, the increase of the potential of you selling the channel as an asset, and obviously the potential of you getting views outside of YouTube. And this is why Sap joined the Art of You Tube program because he wanted to build an asset he could actually own. So, real capital, a sellable asset, the asset that typically sells for uh way more acts than uh just a uh regular commentary channel, uh stable income from a high barrier to entry niche because obviously the higher your barrier to entry, the less competition you have, and uh it means that the more stable your income from YouTube becomes, and the content that actually brings value to the platform because it's not always about money for us. It's uh also about how good is the stuff that we produce. Like, are we proud of what we put out there? Uh these are all of the things that were important for Sap before we actually started. Now, how did he come up with the niche? Because uh the niche was probably uh the main reason how this whole channel exploded. And I have to give all of the credit to uh Sap. Obviously, he knew the uh niche bending framework when you take the format and you bring it to the uh other market. However, the niche itself, uh the way he found it, uh was completely on him. So, what he did is he actually found a format that worked for me for the Art of War channel for uh many other clients of mine, and he brought it to the market where no one has ever uh done any 3D yet. So, in order for you to understand the formula, the way it works is you lock the format and you swap the market. So, uh you take uh something that works here and you bring it there where the target audience has never seen something like that. Uh for example, in this case, uh he took the Zach King Films 3D animated shorts, uh the format that blew up for me, and obviously he also wanted to get into that, and he bent it to the market that was also blowing up. So, the key here is that he used a viral format and brought it to the viral market. So, both pieces of this equation were were absolutely insane and crushing it on YouTube at the time we were choosing a niche. So, he took the Infant's Diary example, the channel that is doing wholesome videos about animals, and at the time the channel was doing about a billion views a month, and he brought the 3D format to that market. And one of the first videos that we ever saw that kind of proved that this idea is going to work is a video from Zach King Films. I should probably fix that here. So, I'm talking about the Zach King Films channel, and on his channel he would sometimes post those wholesome videos, like for example this one where basically a boy was born without an arm, and then he built himself a Lego one, which is very wholesome story. Then also there was a second one about Lego as well, like this one again. Then there were a bunch of others, which basically proved the point that if we bring 3D animated content to this new market, it's going to work. But we went even further, and Sam basically took the inspiration from the subtitles that Infant's Diary were using. We took inspiration from the color grading plot, and by combining the two, we got very unique style of content that did not exist before we actually entered the market. So, 3D shorts bent to wholesome market, and this was the first the most fundamental principle and key to the future success. And this is how our third ever video got 101 million views. So, so if we go to the channel and we filter by the most recent videos, we'll obviously see a bunch of a bunch of videos from before because the channel was pre-monetized, but this is when October 7th, this is when we started posting 3D animated stuff. And as you can see, the third hour video we posted ended up getting 101 million views. So, 101 million views, almost $10,000 in revenue, and the AVD on that video is absolutely insane because it finishes at over 100% view duration, 28 seconds out of 24, and 80% swipe rate. Obviously, those numbers were way higher, but when a video gets 100 million views, it can't hit the perfect threshold all of the time. Now, this is how we structure those videos. This is how they're so good. There is just four key stages that allow us to have that kind of AVD. Number one is the danger, the setup that basically hooks you into the story. So, in this case, the dog charged at the girl in the backyard ready to attack. This is what happened, and this is what makes you stop scrolling instantly. Then, we always include some kind of a pivot, and but just before it did, her brother came running. So, we introduced the hero, and before we obviously introduced the villain. Now, your brain starts to imagine what can happen next. It starts to go in through different scenarios, and while you do that, you keep watching the video. So, this is the magic of all of those scripts. Then, the hero moment, the most wholesome part of the video. He jumped in front of her fighting it off, and the emotional closer. This is why those videos are so good because the emotional part comes at the very end. This is the exact payoff of our videos, and it's something that you would rewatch if you're a fan of this type of content. If something had to die, I'd rather it be me. And after that, you either send this video on WhatsApp to your friend, or you just watch it multiple times, which we see is happening based on those graphs. Now, every script 60 to 120 words, 20 to 30 seconds. We're not creating longer videos because A, we have a payoff at the end and uh we want as many people as possible to see it. And B, because uh those videos are uh 3D animated, it's actually quite expensive to go uh to push for longer. Uh then, obviously, we always use but almost in every video. Uh we use short sentences just so they are very punchy and every sentence is linked to what we said in the previous one. So, we develop like a story. At the end, we always have one-line emotional closers uh that people will quote in the comments or uh rewatch this video um just to see it one more time. No CTAs, nothing. The video ends exactly when the payoff is delivered. And even though we add those real images at the end of our uh videos, we still try to keep them as short as possible because uh your video should end exactly when the payoff uh happens. Now, this is exactly how we scripted uh the best brother ever, the best beluga ever. Uh all of our top-performing videos were scripted uh in the exact same way that I just showed you. 100 million views, 95 million views, 62 million views, 56, 58, 50 million views. All of those videos did generate multiple five figures combined. They were all scripted according to the same framework that you just saw. Now, the real reason why it works is because a viewer gets maximum dopamine per second. The hook instantly intrigues them. Uh the story develops rapidly and uh every single sentence is very short and precise. Uh then, sentences are linked between each other, so they keep delivering new information and they never bore the viewer. And the payoff, obviously, makes you want to rewatch the whole story. Most often, it would reveal the part of the story that uh you could not predict, uh surprising you even more. All of those emotions spark dopamine in your brain, and all of those emotions make you feel those videos um on repeat. And something that I learned myself from the video that Sep did revealing the channel and showing his strategy because in our partnership he's the one in charge of scripts and then charge of ideations well. He mentioned something incredibly important that I wanted to quote here. He said that you should stop blindly staring at the AVD and swipe rate. YouTube tracks hundreds of metrics you cannot possibly analyze all of them. You don't even have all of them in your YouTube studio. Instead, one of them is viewer satisfaction which YouTube will never show you. You should optimize for views. This is the most important metric, not the CTR, not the AVD and swipe rate that everybody else is talking about because because some of our best performing videos are actually quite average in terms of the swipe rate and AVD. And if we were to blindly optimize for that, then we would just keep replicating videos that does not make sense to replicate. So, focus on the views and optimize for stories, not for surface metrics that you can see from your YouTube studio. And this is the exact same framework that we put into each one of our videos since the very beginning. Video 1, 14 million views. Video 2, 15 million views. Video 3, 100 million views. Since the very beginning we were using that. Back then, we didn't have it pretty much figured out. We were just trying and seeing what works best. Then when we started dialing it in, obviously channel exploded and got to the consistent 15 to 20K months. But, unfortunately right after that it dipped. For 45 days the channel was absolutely still. It was in February and we were quite seriously questioning what is going on, whether the niche died out or something else has happened. This was at the exact same time that Infin's Diary channel, the one that I was showing you at the very beginning died as well, which again always happens to commentary channels because their niches just get flooded with competitors and get overcrowded quickly. We could not understand why it happened to our 3D animated channel because at that time we only had one competitor and he was not even posting consistently. So, this is the difference. If you compare our channel to Infin's Diary, the moment they exploded they started getting dozen of competitors every single day. Till this day, our channel has only one or maybe two competitors and none of them are actually serious and posting the volume that we do, again, because of how hard it actually is to produce our videos. And this is the exact dip that I'm talking about. As you can see, the channel was growing steadily right until February when it dipped and we lost half of our viewership. But, one thing that kept us going and helped us survive that and the key fundamental principle number two that allowed the channel to be successful is how much we pay for our videos because compared to our main enemy and competitor Zac the Films who's paying a thousand bucks per one YouTube short, we were able to find a team and pay them only $150 per video. Because we hire high ROI teams instead of working with freelancers, we hire from CIS markets instead of looking for Indian or Pakistani talent like many other people do. And we hire on Telegram instead of Upwork and Fiverr. Because of all three of those reasons all together, we were able to find a team that produces more videos for cheaper at the same quality. And this was the cheat code because obviously when you pay ten times less than your competition or even five times less than your competition, you have a massive advantage of how much more content you can produce and how much longer you can survive the dips because the dips happen to almost every short-form channel. The only thing that kept us going is that we were still profitable even when we lost half of our viewership. And the key here is you need to hire your animators from Eastern Europe Europe, not on Upwork, not on Fiverr, none of those platforms have the high-quality talent you're searching for. Most of the time they overcharge for very basic simple services. I got scammed on Upwork so many times. Some of those freelancers took our channel principles and guidelines and then went ahead and started their own channels. Some of them never delivered, always postponed deadlines, and came up with excuses. If you ever hired on Upwork, you know what I'm talking about. That's not the place where you can find reliable team members. Instead, you should be aiming for Eastern European markets. 1.2, 1.5 K a month. This is the salary that these people would expect, and which makes them 50-70% cheaper than US talent, and they are actually way more reliable than freelancers. So, after watching this video, you basically have two path. You have simple niches, which many of you are already searching for. You watch all of those tutorials to find the easiest the the cheapest AI niche ever possible. Like you look for ways to automate your video production with a single tool to start with no capital, but it has problems because those niches they get saturated in two to three months. Most of you act too slow, and you can't even dominate this niche. While you are still figuring out the AI tool, whatever, some operators are crushing you by posting 15 videos in a single day. If you can't do the volume, the simple niche is going to destroy you because there are operators who are actually throwing hundreds of people NBA and their systems, actual systems, uh to find those niches, to spot them early, and then to dominate them. Uh obviously, in this niche, uh in a simple niche, you would always have hundreds of competitors, you will have no barrier to entry. And if any kid can replicate your style of content, then basically expect the niche to die out within the next 2 months. And uh yes, you can get that 12x multiple if you uh sell the channel, uh but this is the race to the bottom, essentially. The branded niche, the high barrier to entry niche, on the other hand, uh requires pretty much some uh capital. If you know how to hire, it's not going to be much. It's going to be 1, 1.5 K a month. You can start slow. Sapnap only started with 1.2 K a month budget, and we only spent it uh on one single animator. Right now, he operates a team of 10 people, right? Obviously, it survives long-term. Uh you get up to five competitors, if any, uh in 9 months. It has a high barrier to entry, which is your moat. And uh it has a way higher multiple uh than um a typical commentary or simple niche. And it's a compounding asset that you build for life. Now, in order for you to build something like that, many people would assume that it's something complicated or something hard. But in reality, for Sapnap, this is how his pipeline looks. His ideas are generated by AI in a combination with him. So, Sapnap assists that process. He uh obviously uses all of the AI tools uh to help him uh come up with as many ideas as we need. Then, obviously, there are script-writing AIs that uh are doing the uh perfect job at writing those scripts for him. There is a team that runs the animation side of it. So, basically all of the animators are hands off for Sap. They're just doing their job according to the quality guidelines that we've built. Even the production is hands off because we have a channel manager baby-sitting each one of those employees himself and Sap never touches it. And we have the key part of the process fully on Sap, which is analysis and decisions. This is where you want to be as an operator. As a faceless YouTube operator, you want yourself to be able to only be on that step and fully divide yourself to that one thing. You look at the stats daily. You explore what are the other niches and opportunities out there. You look at the competitors and what they are doing. You're always in the trenches with the team. However, you're not baby-sitting them and you're not doing the work that can be automated. You're there as a decision maker. And this is exactly how it looks for Sap. Now, there's five things I want you to learn after watching this video. Number one, niche band. Never start a channel if there's already someone doing the exact same thing that you're planning to. Never copy. Never steal. Instead, niche band and come up with something new. Take the format from one market and bring it to another. Number two, the higher your barrier to entry, the longer your niche is going to survive and the more protected your income is going to be from competition. Number three, team is better than talent because the team stays with you while your talent leaves for a better pay. So, always choose high ROI teams over freelancers. Number four, stories matter more than metrics. The moment we started focusing on how good are the stories that we tell, our channel exploded again. And therefore, the entire period when we over obsessed over swipe rates and AVDs, the channel was struggling. Number five, built to keep. YouTube is one of the only small businesses that you can actually sell or monetize as semi-passive income. So, it doesn't really matter at which stage of your journey you are, always think long-term. Think in 12 months from now. How do you see your channel? How do you see yourself running network of channels or running just one channel and dividing your entire attention there or selling the channel for multiple six figures and the living life. Whatever it is that your long-term goal, make sure that you're actually on track to that by checking all of the four previous boxes. And this is how you build long-term. And obviously, it's proven by this channel, which is a second channel from our portfolio to cross 30K a month mark, 1.4 million views, and 130K in revenue with 1 million followers that we crossed recently. Now, I'm taking on seven new clients this month. Inside of the Art of YouTube program, this is the exact program that Sep went through. This is where he learned how to find a niche, create the niche with the niche band, find ideas, write scripts. We did daily script reviews of his videos, and then we helped him hire an entire team. This is how he started, and as you can see here, this is how it goes for him right now. So, if you want to get the same playbook, work with me one-on-one, get access to my HR and our entire SOPs that I use to run my channels and that Sep currently is using to run his channel, then the way for you to unlock it is the Art of YouTube. The first link in the description will take you there. This is where you get the niche bending AI, our unique software that allows you to come up with blue oceans for yourself, cloud code protocols that will run your ideation, scripting pretty much like they run it for Sep. Team that you will build together with me and my HR will help you hire hire right talent from seas markets that are typically locked for the entire world. We have access to those markets. We'll provide you with that access, too. Uh number four, one-on-one game plan call with me where we will verify your niche, make sure that uh your channel has the potential to go to billions of views and hundreds of thousands of dollars in revenue. And what I'm going to show you how to script bands so that you can also expect hundreds of millions of views across all of our videos. Now, if you decide to do that, then the first thing in the description will take you to the Art of YouTube website where you can book a free 30-minutes uh call with the head of client strategy at The Art of YouTube. This is where you get the niche validation tool and the uh YouTube profit calculator. Incredibly valuable asset that uh you will use for the rest of your um YouTube journey and uh hopefully for the rest of your life if um YouTube is something that you want to do long-term. And uh uh my head of client strategy will point out at the holes in your current uh setup and walk you through what fixing it look like. Uh link is in the description and in a pinned comment if you want to do that. Uh and uh uh the next case study that I post on this channel could be yours.
